title: Italian DPA orders urgent provisional limitation on ChatGPT personal-data processing in Italy
summary: Authoritative regulator order concerning ChatGPT data-processing practices, framed by the Italian DPA as an urgent provisional limitation pending further inquiry.

actors.name: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali

source_claims.claim: The Italian DPA ordered an urgent provisional limitation on OpenAI's processing of personal data through ChatGPT for data subjects in Italy and listed information, legal-basis, accuracy, and age-verification concerns in the order text.

legal_normative_notes: Use the regulator's own language: urgent provisional limitation, not a generalized finding of illegality beyond the order text.
Italian original should be preserved for citation and legal nuance review.
remediation_or_response: Order invited OpenAI to communicate initiatives taken within 20 days to implement the measure and justify the issues identified in the order.

review_notes: Authoritative source only this cycle; no independent court or company-notice supplement added.
Current source package includes direct Garante order text on the regulator site; use as the lane's current document-depth benchmark.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Italian original should remain the authoritative wording for legal nuance review.

title: EEOC sues iTutorGroup over age-discriminatory automated tutor-screening software
summary: Authoritative EEOC case package covering both the 2022 lawsuit filing posture and the 2023 settlement/decree posture in the iTutorGroup automated hiring discrimination matter, but still without open court-originating complaint/decree text captured on this host.

source_claims.claim: The EEOC stated that iTutorGroup programmed application software to automatically reject female applicants aged 55+ and male applicants aged 60+, rejected more than 200 qualified U.S.-based applicants, and filed suit in federal court.

The EEOC stated that iTutorGroup would pay $365,000 to settle the suit and that the decree includes anti-discrimination policy changes, hiring-related training, injunctions against discriminatory hiring based on age or sex and requests for birth dates, and compliance monitoring if U.S. operations resume.

legal_normative_notes: Do not convert the EEOC allegation into a final judicial finding; the press release states the agency filed suit.
A court-document supplement would strengthen court-grade procedural tracking.
The settlement/decree posture strengthens procedural support for this case, but the EEOC press release remains an agency summary rather than a standalone full docket history.
Use the progression carefully: allegation and filing in 2022, settlement/decree summary in 2023; avoid implying a merits judgment beyond what the source says.
remediation_or_response: EEOC said it sought back pay, liquidated damages, and injunctive relief.
EEOC said the decree settling the suit provides $365,000 to applicants automatically rejected due to age.

EEOC said the decree includes training, a new anti-discrimination policy, injunctions against discriminatory hiring and birth-date requests, and compliance monitoring tied to any resumed U.S. operations.

review_notes: Agency press release used as authoritative source; underlying district-court filings remain a next-step verification target.
Settlement/decree follow-up added from a second EEOC press release to make the procedural progression reviewer-visible without asserting a broader judicial merits finding.
Current source package remains agency-summary-level; no official court-docket filing, order, or decree text was captured this cycle.
A bounded second-pass public-court-surface probe checked CourtListener API/web, Justia, and the official EDNY iQuery entry point without capturing open complaint, decree, docket, or order text.
The current blocker is now better specified as a mix of public-mirror access barriers and PACER-gated official-court access rather than an untried obvious route.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Do not promote to quote-ready court anchor until an open complaint, decree, docket entry, or order text is captured.

court_surface_probe.probe_note: Bounded second-pass checks across CourtListener API/web, Justia, and the official EDNY iQuery entry point yielded only authentication, web-access, or PACER-login barriers and no open court-originating text.

title: UK ICO orders Serco Leisure and associated trusts to stop biometric attendance monitoring
summary: Authoritative UK regulator enforcement package with direct enforcement-notice text concerning facial recognition and fingerprint scanning used for employee attendance monitoring.

actors.name: Serco Leisure Operating Limited and associated trusts

system_context.model_or_provider: facial recognition technology and fingerprint scanning system

source_claims.claim: The ICO stated it issued enforcement notices ordering Serco Leisure, Serco Jersey and associated trusts to stop processing biometric data for attendance monitoring and to destroy biometric data they were not legally obliged to retain.

The ICO's enforcement-action record classifies the Serco matter as enforcement notices and states that the regulator found unlawful biometric-data processing affecting more than 2,000 employees across 38 leisure facilities for attendance monitoring.

The ICO enforcement notice PDF states that the notice was issued under DPA 2018 section 149(2)(a) and (c) in relation to contraventions of Articles 5(1)(a), 6, and 9 UK GDPR, and Annex 1 requires Serco within three months to cease biometric attendance-check processing at relevant facilities and destroy biometric and other personal/special-category data not legally required to be retained.

legal_normative_notes: Keep to the ICO's proportionality/fairness framing and enforcement-notice description; do not generalize beyond the notice summary page.

The enforcement-action page adds bounded scale/detail (more than 2,000 employees; 38 facilities) and record type (enforcement notices) without changing the need to avoid broader legality or sector-prevalence claims.
Direct notice text is now locally captured, so narrow claims can cite the ICO notice itself rather than only the newsroom and enforcement-record summaries.
remediation_or_response: The ICO notice requires Serco within three months to cease biometric attendance-check processing at relevant facilities and destroy biometric and other personal/special-category data not legally required to be retained.

review_notes: Authoritative regulator summary page captured; underlying enforcement notices could be added later as document-level supplements.
Current source package includes ICO newsroom and enforcement-record summaries, but no linked enforcement-notice PDF or notice-text page was captured this cycle.
Current source package now includes the linked ICO enforcement notice PDF with direct notice text and remedy terms.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Anchor wording should stay tied to the enforcement notice and Annex 1 remedy language.

title: UK ICO reprimands Essex school over facial-recognition canteen payments
summary: Authoritative UK regulator reprimand package with direct reprimand text concerning school use of facial recognition technology for student canteen payments.

source_claims.claim: The ICO stated it reprimanded Chelmer Valley High School after the school introduced facial recognition technology for canteen payments without first carrying out a DPIA and without properly obtaining explicit consent for students' biometric information.

The ICO enforcement-action record classifies the Chelmer Valley High School matter as a reprimand in respect of Article 35(1) and states that the school failed to complete a DPIA before introducing facial recognition for cashless catering.

The ICO reprimand PDF states that the Commissioner issued a reprimand under Article 58(2)(b) UK GDPR in respect of Article 35(1), that Chelmer Valley High School introduced facial recognition in March 2023 without a prior DPIA, and that from March to November 2023 the controller relied on assumed opt-out consent that would not have been valid or lawful consent for the processing.

legal_normative_notes: The source frames this as a reprimand and data-protection failure; avoid inferring broader school-sector prevalence from one case.
Student/minor context warrants metadata-first treatment.
The enforcement-action page provides the narrower legal framing of a reprimand in respect of Article 35(1) tied to failure to complete a DPIA before introduction.
The linked newsroom page is dated 2024-07-23 while the enforcement-action record is dated 2024-07-22; keep both dates explicit rather than collapsing them.
Direct reprimand text is now locally captured, which supports narrow citation of the ICO reprimand itself while still avoiding broader school-sector inference.
remediation_or_response: The reprimand records that the school later completed a DPIA in November 2023 and that the ICO welcomed some remedial steps while still issuing the reprimand.

review_notes: Authority is strong for the reprimand summary page; no pupil-level details were retained.
Current source package includes ICO newsroom and enforcement-record summaries, but no linked reprimand notice PDF or notice-text page was captured this cycle.
Current source package now includes the linked ICO reprimand PDF with direct reprimand text and process-failure detail.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Minors context should remain metadata-first and sparingly quoted.

title: DOJ charges Nate founder over alleged AI investment-fraud misstatements and hidden human transaction processing
summary: Authoritative DOJ charging-stage package with a linked indictment PDF concerning alleged false statements about nate's proprietary AI technology and operational capabilities.

actors.name: U.S. Attorney's Office, Southern District of New York

system_context.model_or_provider: nate app / purported proprietary AI technology

source_claims.claim: The DOJ press release states that an indictment charged the former Nate CEO with allegedly making false and misleading statements about nate's proprietary AI technology and operational capabilities, and that the charges are accusations only.

The DOJ press release states that nate relied heavily on human workers, including hundreds of contractors in the Philippines, to manually process transactions that users believed were being done by automation.
A DOJ-linked indictment PDF was publicly captured and hashed as the underlying charging document for the Nate matter.

legal_normative_notes: Use this row as charging-stage authority only; do not convert the indictment allegations into adjudicated fact or liability.
Carry the DOJ presumption-of-innocence sentence with any manuscript or dashboard reuse.
The linked indictment PDF improves court-facing document presence, but exact caption/case-number extraction still remains a next-step normalization task.
remediation_or_response: No adjudicated remedy, conviction, or regulator-final sanction is represented in the local package yet; current support is charging-stage only.

review_notes: Source package combines a DOJ charging-stage summary page with a captured DOJ-linked indictment PDF.
Current use remains accusation-bounded and should not be promoted to adjudicated, regulator-final, or sector-general evidence.
Exact court caption/case-number extraction from the indictment PDF remains a next-step normalization target.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Summary-level accusation-stage wording is usable; exact caption metadata remains blocked.

title: SEC settlement with Delphia over false and misleading statements about AI and machine learning use
summary: The SEC found that Delphia made false and misleading statements in filings, advertisements, and social media about using artificial intelligence and machine learning with client data in its investment process.

source_claims.claim: The SEC announced settled charges against Delphia for making false and misleading statements about its purported use of AI and machine learning.

The SEC order states that from at least August 2019 to August 2023 Delphia represented that it used AI and machine learning to analyze retail clients' spending and social media data when no such data was being used in its investment process.

legal_normative_notes: Use as an SEC enforcement finding and settlement record, not as a judicial determination of broader fraud facts.
Good paper-grade example of AI capability exaggeration in regulated financial advice marketing.
remediation_or_response: SEC censure and cease-and-desist order
Civil penalty: USD 225000

review_notes: Authoritative single-regulator package; no independent corroboration added this cycle because the sprint favored exact authoritative support over breadth.
Promoted from shard-local SEC package into the shared authoritative ledger so the main regulator/enforcement atlas includes settled AI-washing order exemplars.

title: SEC settlement with Global Predictions over false and misleading AI financial-adviser claims

The SEC order states that Global Predictions made false and misleading claims about its use of AI, its status as the first regulated AI financial advisor, and the services it offered.

legal_normative_notes: Use as an SEC enforcement finding and settlement record, not as proof that all AI-enabled advisory products are similarly misleading.
Useful comparator case for paper tables on AI washing in regulated sectors.
remediation_or_response: SEC censure and cease-and-desist order
Civil penalty: USD 175000

review_notes: Authoritative single-regulator package; further crosswalking to AIID/OECD/AVID remains open.

title: FCC pursues and settles Lingo Telecom deepfake robocall enforcement over New Hampshire primary spoofing
summary: Authoritative FCC enforcement package spanning allegation-stage and consent-decree stages for spoofed robocalls that used a generative AI voice clone in connection with the 2024 New Hampshire presidential primary.

system_context.model_or_provider: generative AI voice cloning used in spoofed robocalls

source_claims.claim: The FCC Notice of Apparent Liability states that Lingo transmitted spoofed robocalls carrying a deepfake generative-AI voice message purporting to be from the President of the United States, targeted New Hampshire primary voters, and proposes a $2,000,000 penalty for incorrect STIR/SHAKEN attestations.
The FCC order states that Lingo Telecom entered into a Consent Decree, agreed to implement a robust compliance plan, and agreed to pay a $1,000,000 civil penalty to resolve the investigation.

The FCC media release states that the settlement concerned spoofed robocalls using generative AI voice cloning technology to spread disinformation in connection with the New Hampshire presidential primary and describes the compliance plan as first-of-its-kind.

legal_normative_notes: Keep allegation-stage and consent-decree stages distinct: the May 2024 FCC Notice of Apparent Liability is not a final adjudication, while the August 2024 order resolves the matter through a Consent Decree and civil penalty.

Use the FCC documents for narrow claims about spoofed deepfake robocalls, STIR/SHAKEN attestation posture, and compliance-plan remedies; do not convert this record into a general election-deepfake prevalence claim.
The case strengthens deepfake/synthetic-identity authority depth in LIMEN, but it remains a telecom-enforcement exemplar rather than a general content-authenticity verification result.
remediation_or_response: The FCC order states that Lingo Telecom agreed to a robust compliance plan and a $1,000,000 civil penalty through a Consent Decree resolving the investigation.

review_notes: Source package combines an allegation-stage FCC Notice of Apparent Liability, a disposition-stage FCC order, and an FCC media release.
The row is strong for telecom/deepfake enforcement posture and remedy language, but should not be generalized into broad election-deepfake incidence claims.
The official FCC text gives direct document-grade support for both the proposed-penalty and settled-penalty stages.
2026-06-07 bounded AIID overlap pilot verified against public AIID incident 628 via search route and page-data JSON.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Use the FCC NAL for allegation-stage wording and the FCC order for settlement/compliance-plan wording; keep the posture transition explicit.

title: Slovenian police ORION page describes AI-assisted border-management pilot with encrypted-biometric-processing language
summary: Authoritative Slovenian police page plus official EU CORDIS fact-sheet HTML, structured XML, and POST-backed PDF fact-sheet companions for Project ORION, usable as a bounded multilingual public-sector governance exemplar with explicit pilot-stage and deployment-status caution.

actors.name: Policija / Ministry of the Interior, Police

system_context.model_or_provider: Project ORION border-management technologies and recommendation systems
tools_or_permissions: search and processing of encrypted biometric data

source_claims.claim: The Slovenian police page states that Project ORION aims to improve border management with the help of artificial intelligence and frames the target border-control approach as secure, reliable, and privacy-preserving.

The page lists six sub-goals including interoperability with SIS, VIS, and EES, resource-allocation optimization, threat-detection improvement, and ethical/legal compliance plus passenger-data protection.

The page says the project develops technologies toward TRL 7, mentions privacy-preserving systems able to search and process encrypted biometric data, and describes pilot scenarios in Romania, Bulgaria, and Slovenia, with the Slovenian scenario framed as improved risk assessment and resource allocation at land border crossings.

The official CORDIS fact sheet identifies ORION as grant agreement 101225611, coordinated by UBITECH LIMITED in Cyprus, funded under Civil Security for Society, and receiving EUR 5,240,093.75 in EU contribution.

The CORDIS objective text states that ORION aims to improve coordination between border, customs, and security controls, dynamically optimize border-check resource allocation, enforce interoperability with existing databases and legacy systems, and demonstrate four use cases including enhanced risk assessment and resource allocation at land border check points.

The CORDIS participant list publicly includes UNIVERZA V MARIBORU in Slovenia, INSPECTORATUL TERITORIAL AL POLITIEI DE FRONTIERA IASI in Romania, and GLAVNA DIREKTSIA GRANICHNA POLITSIA in Bulgaria, strengthening the bounded multi-country public-authority companion chain for the ORION row.

The official CORDIS XML record exposes machine-readable ORION metadata including grant agreement ID 101225611, acronym ORION, status SIGNED, ecSignatureDate 2025-07-16, start date 2025-09-01, end date 2028-08-31, and EUR 5,240,093.75 EU contribution.

The XML organization nodes list public-authority participant names including MINISTRY OF THE INTERIOR and border-police bodies, preserving the bounded multi-public-authority participant chain in machine-readable form.

The official CORDIS PDF fact sheet confirms ORION title, grant agreement ID 101225611, DOI 10.3030/101225611, coordinator UBITECH LIMITED, EC signature date 16 July 2025, start date 1 September 2025, end date 31 August 2028, and EU contribution € 5 240 093,75.

legal_normative_notes: This row is an official project-description page, not an enforcement action, judicial finding, procurement award, or external audit.
Translation caution remains active: keep Slovenian original phrases available for reviewer or human legal/policy review before any stronger normative interpretation.

Use this row for bounded claims about project existence, stated purpose, technical/governance language, and pilot framing only; do not overread it as proof of production deployment, legality, proportionality, effectiveness, or independent compliance validation.

The English-language CORDIS fact sheet lowers translation burden for project-structure and objective metadata, but it still does not independently validate legality, proportionality, effectiveness, procurement outcome, or production deployment.

The structured CORDIS XML companion improves reproducible metadata capture and graph-ready participant extraction, but it still does not validate legality, procurement outcome, effectiveness, or production deployment.
remediation_or_response: No sanction or corrective remedy is at issue in the current source package; the visible official surface is a project-description and pilot-framing page.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Use as a bounded multilingual official-row exemplar with explicit translation caution and project-stage ceiling; prefer paraphrase over quote-heavy reuse until human language review is available.

CORDIS can anchor English-language metadata about grant ID, coordinator, funding line, and objective/use-case structure, while the Slovene police page remains the narrower direct source for certain local framing phrases.
The XML route is useful when a methods table, dashboard, or appendix needs machine-readable proof of official metadata and participant-chain extraction rather than prose-only fact-sheet support.

review_notes: Promoted from multilingual row LMWCS-20260606-004 because the official Slovenian police page is already directly resolved and is the strongest current non-U.S./non-UK authority-diversification route.
Current support is official project-page text with machine-assisted translation plus local bounded extraction notes, not independent audit or deployment validation.

The promotion is useful for geography diversification and public-sector/policing governance coverage, but the claim ceiling remains lower than regulator-order or court-document exemplars already in the package.

Added an official English-language EU CORDIS fact-sheet companion that confirms project identifier, coordinator, funding line, objective structure, and public-authority participant chain without changing the bounded project-stage claim ceiling.

Added the official machine-readable CORDIS XML companion to preserve exact status/signature-date metadata and graph-ready participant extraction without changing the bounded project-stage claim ceiling.

Added the official CORDIS PDF fact-sheet export companion and verified exact project metadata (grant agreement ID, DOI, coordinator, signature/start/end dates, and EU contribution) without changing the bounded project-stage claim ceiling.

title: FTC final order and complaint package on DoNotPay AI-lawyer and robot-lawyer claims
summary: Official FTC complaint-plus-final-order package on DoNotPay's AI-lawyer / robot-lawyer capability claims, now promoted into the shared authoritative core as a bounded non-finance official AI-claim exemplar rather than a judicial unauthorized-practice finding.

system_context.model_or_provider: DoNotPay service / robot lawyer / AI lawyer marketing claims

source_claims.claim: The FTC complaint states that DoNotPay described its service as the world's first robot lawyer and an AI lawyer capable of drafting ironclad demand letters, contracts, and small-claims complaints, while alleging that the service was not designed to operate like a human lawyer in the advertised respects.

The FTC final order bars DoNotPay from representing that a covered product or service operates like a human lawyer by applying relevant laws to subscribers' particular legal and factual situations and orders USD 193000 in monetary relief.

legal_normative_notes: Use FTC complaint/final-order posture only; do not restate the row as a judicial unauthorized-practice finding, adjudicated product-truth finding, or general legal-services automation verdict.
The row is strongest as a bounded non-finance official AI-claim exemplar that widens authority diversification beyond settled SEC finance matters.
Keep complaint allegations and final-order prohibitions distinct when reusing this package in manuscript, dashboard, or thesis prose.
remediation_or_response: The FTC final order imposes prohibitions on misleading or unsubstantiated human-lawyer-equivalence claims, other misrepresentations about covered services, customer-notice duties, compliance-report duties, and USD 193000 in monetary relief.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Use the complaint for robot-lawyer / AI-lawyer marketed-capability wording and the final order for human-lawyer-equivalence prohibitions plus USD 193000 monetary relief.
Keep FTC complaint-versus-final-order posture explicit and do not overread the package into unauthorized-practice adjudication, hidden-human closure, or cross-sector prevalence.

review_notes: Promoted from the FTC frontier after bounded local reading of both the complaint and final-order PDFs captured under results/boost/limen-boost-021/tmp/ftc/.
This row is suitable for reviewer-safe non-finance AI-claim diversification, but not for unauthorized-practice or product-truth overread.

title: FTC final order and complaint package on Workado / Content at Scale AI detector accuracy claims
summary: Official FTC complaint-plus-final-order package on Workado / Content at Scale AI detector-accuracy advertising, now promoted into the shared authoritative core as a bounded detector-governance and false-capability row.

system_context.model_or_provider: AI Content Detector / AI Content Checker

source_claims.claim: The FTC complaint states that Workado claimed its AI Content Detector could predict with 98.3% accuracy whether text was generated using systems such as ChatGPT, GPT-4, Claude, and Bard, while alleging that the model was not tested for the plain-language text users typically submitted and that public results showed substantially lower performance than 98.3%.

The FTC final order bars Workado from making misleading or unsubstantiated representations about an AI-detection product's effectiveness and imposes testing-record preservation and customer-notice obligations.

legal_normative_notes: Use FTC complaint/final-order posture only; do not convert this row into a general scientific-validity conclusion about all AI detectors or a prevalence claim about detector fraud.

The row is strongest as a bounded detector-governance / false-capability exemplar that can bridge AI-misstatement evidence with research-integrity and educational-process caution without overclaiming causality.
Keep the complaint's performance allegations distinct from the final order's substantiation and recordkeeping requirements.
remediation_or_response: The FTC final order prohibits misleading or unsubstantiated effectiveness claims about AI-detection products and requires preservation of supporting testing records plus customer-notice obligations.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Use the complaint for the 98.3% detector-accuracy claim and plain-language-testing gap, and the final order for substantiation, evidence-retention, and customer-notice duties.

Shared-core reuse is now allowed for bounded detector-governance / false-capability narration, but keep the narrower scientific-overread guardrail explicit on every manuscript, dashboard, or thesis surface.

review_notes: Promoted from the FTC frontier after bounded local reading of both the complaint and final-order PDFs captured under results/boost/limen-boost-021/tmp/ftc/.
This row is suitable for detector-governance and false-capability discussion, but not for generalized scientific claims about all detectors.

title: Datatilsynet development-and-drift opinions on AI-supported screening of disability-allowance documentation in Danish student-aid administration
summary: Official Danish regulator development-and-drift page pair plus a case-specific legal-basis chain and official UFS taskforce report, with live readable Retsinformation legal text confirming both the row-specific disability-allowance screening context and a broader ministerial authorization for AI or similar digital solutions in SU applications and related case handling, preserved as a rare-language public-sector governance anchor rather than a harm or deployment finding.

system_context.model_or_provider: AI-løsning for screening of handicaptillæg documentation

source_claims.claim: The Datatilsynet page states that, after a request from the Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science, the authority assessed whether the agency had a legal basis to develop and operate an AI solution to support review of disability-allowance applications.
The page states that the personal-data processing could in principle rest on GDPR Article 6(1)(e) and Article 9(2)(g), provided there is supplementary national legal basis.

The page states that the AI system was described as being used only for initial screening of whether sufficient documentation was attached, not as final decision support on disability-allowance entitlement, and therefore not as directly affecting the applicant's possibility of receiving the allowance.

The 2025 Datatilsynet drift page keeps the same journal number and states that operation of the AI solution can rely on GDPR Article 6(1)(e) and Article 9(2)(g) together with SU-loven, SU-bekendtgørelsen, and AI-SU-bekendtgørelsen, while also naming storage/deletion, security, design/defaults, and impact-analysis safeguards.

The AI-SU ministerial regulation states that the styrelsen may use artificial intelligence or similar digital solutions to process personal data in connection with SU applications and related case handling when necessary to support administration, and it lists purpose, data categories, security obligations, publication duties, and allowed information sources.

The June 2025 SU-law amendment inserted Section 39 a, authorizing the minister to issue rules on the use of artificial intelligence or similar digital solutions to support case handling under the SU law, including related personal-data processing.

The official March 2024 UFS taskforce report states that UFS was exploring whether artificial intelligence could support case handling, had entered into cooperation with a supplier to explore automated screening of incoming handicaptillæg applications, and was awaiting a guiding opinion from Datatilsynet before further development could proceed.

legal_normative_notes: Preserve Danish original and treat any English gloss as a translation-aided paraphrase rather than a final legal interpretation.
Use the page as a governance/legal-basis anchor only; it is not, by itself, evidence of harm, illegality, deployment failure, or compliance certification.

A 2025 same-row Datatilsynet drift page now provides exact-row follow-through beyond the 2024 development page, but it still supports governance/legal-basis claims rather than procurement proof, impact evidence, or general legality conclusions.

The Danish row now has a specific statutory-plus-regulatory companion chain (Section 39 a plus BEK nr 813), but this remains governance/legal-basis support rather than procurement, evaluation, impact, harm, or compliance proof.
remediation_or_response: The page frames the regulator's intervention as legal-basis and governance analysis for planned development and operation, not as punitive enforcement or post-harm remediation.

The 2025 drift page points to AI-SU-bekendtgørelsen-level conditions and to safeguards on storage/deletion, security, design/defaults, and impact analysis as part of the legal-basis frame for operation.
observatory_hooks: source-depth ladder note for public-sector decision-support rows

provenance.rights_terms_note: Public regulator page; preserve Danish original and quote sparingly with translation caution.
review_notes: 2025 same-row drift page added as exact-row follow-through; keep procurement/DPIA/evaluation gap explicit despite stronger governance continuity.

Added a case-specific Danish legal-basis companion chain from Retsinformation: the June 2025 AI-SU ministerial regulation and the SU-law amendment inserting Section 39 a. This strengthens exact-row governance depth without converting the row into procurement, audit, impact, harm, or compliance evidence.

2026-06-08 live browser rendering of Retsinformation BEK nr 813 exposed the operative legal text on-host, confirming exact readable support for §1 authorization, §1 stk.2 optimization purpose, and §6 su.dk publication duty after earlier shell-only GET friction.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Use only for legal-basis/governance and non-final-screening posture claims from the Danish regulator page.
Preserve translation caution and keep the row outside harm/enforcement denominators.

notes: Reusable as a bounded rare-language public-sector governance sidecar in shared dashboard/manuscript surfaces without changing the shared authoritative denominator.
Keep Danish-original-preserving paraphrase discipline and do not restate the row as a harm finding, procurement proof, or compliance certification.
shared_reuse_state.promotion_blocker: The row now has exact-row development-to-drift continuity, but it still lacks procurement, DPIA, evaluation, or court-facing depth and therefore remains best used as a bounded governance sidecar rather than a core enforcement comparator.

title: FTC Rytr complaint-final-order-set-aside chronology on AI-generated reviews, retained as a complex-posture negative result
summary: Official FTC complaint, 2024 final-order, and 2025 set-aside chronology for Rytr's AI review/testimonial generator, now fully document-backed on this host and usable as a bounded regulatory-reversibility / complex-posture negative result rather than a stable current-order exemplar.

system_context.model_or_provider: Rytr writing service / Testimonial & Review use case

source_claims.claim: The FTC complaint states that Rytr sold a generative-AI writing service with a Testimonial & Review use case and alleges that the service generated detailed reviews with little user input, often including material details unrelated to the prompts.

The FTC complaint alleges that the service causes or is likely to cause substantial consumer harm by facilitating fake reviews and that at least some subscribers generated hundreds, thousands, or tens of thousands of reviews.

The FTC final decision and order states that Rytr must not advertise, market, promote, offer for sale, or sell any review or testimonial generation service and imposes compliance and recordkeeping obligations.

The FTC order reopening and setting aside the Rytr final order states that the Commission reopened and set aside the 2024 final order, stated that the specific facts in the complaint do not support a Section 5 violation, and recorded that Rytr consented to vacating the order.

legal_normative_notes: Use the full complaint/final-order/set-aside chronology together; do not reuse this row as a stable current final-order exemplar after the 2025 reopening and set-aside order.

This row is now strongest as a bounded official regulatory-reversibility / complex-posture negative result for review-manipulation governance, not as a denominator-widening authoritative-core promotion.
Do not convert the FTC's set-aside reasoning into a general scientific or legal vindication claim beyond the order text.
remediation_or_response: The 2024 FTC final order had barred Rytr from offering review or testimonial generation services, but the later 2025 FTC order reopened and set aside that final order and recorded Rytr's consent to vacatur.

manuscript_anchor.manuscript_anchor_note: Chronology is now document-complete on this host, but the set-aside posture makes the row suitable only for limitations/reversibility/appendix use unless a deliberate shared-sidecar rewrite is justified.

title: FTC Air AI complaint-TRO-stipulated-order package on conversational-AI business-opportunity marketing, now disposition-backed but kept non-core
summary: Official FTC complaint, TRO-motion, and March 2026 stipulated-order package on Air AI's conversational-AI business-opportunity marketing, now direct-document-backed through disposition on this host and usable as a bounded settlement-sidecar rather than a title-only or complaint-only queue row.

system_context.model_or_provider: Air AI conversational AI / Odin / Max sales-calling suite

source_claims.claim: The FTC complaint alleges that Air AI defendants sold business coaching, licensing, and an AI-related product suite marketed around conversational AI that would allegedly replace human sales or customer-service functions.

The FTC complaint alleges deceptive earnings claims, deceptive refund claims, and violations of the Business Opportunity Rule and Telemarketing Sales Rule in connection with the Air AI Access Card and licensing business opportunity.
The FTC publicly filed a same-day motion for a temporary restraining order and related relief in the Air AI matter, adding procedural-document depth beyond the blocked case-page shell.

The stipulated order states that the complaint charged deceptive acts or practices under the FTC Act, Telemarketing Sales Rule, and Business Opportunity Rule; that defendants neither admit nor deny the allegations except jurisdiction facts; that defendants are permanently banned from creating, advertising, marketing, distributing, promoting, offering for sale, or selling any Business Opportunity; and that an $18,000,000 judgment was entered with $50,000 due and the remainder suspended subject to financial representations.

legal_normative_notes: Use complaint-plus-stipulated-order posture together and preserve the order's non-admission clause; do not turn this into a hidden-human finding or a general AI-washing prevalence claim.

This row is now strong enough for a bounded non-core settlement sidecar on conversational-AI business-opportunity / human-replacement marketing governance, but it still does not outrank the authoritative core's sf08 court/public-record blocker.
The order text supports a business-opportunity ban and monetary-judgment posture; it does not itself prove technical capability, system efficacy, or broader market prevalence.
remediation_or_response: The stipulated order permanently bans defendants from creating, advertising, marketing, distributing, promoting, offering for sale, or selling any Business Opportunity, enters an $18,000,000 judgment, orders payment of $50,000, and suspends the remainder subject to financial representations.

manuscript_anchor.manuscript_anchor_note: Disposition is now captured, so the row is no longer complaint-only; use as a bounded settlement-sidecar unless a deliberate authoritative-core rewrite is justified.

provenance.route_note: Host-local FTC legal-library shell was anti-automation blocked; direct FTC PDF URLs were reachable and hashed.

shared_reuse_state.promotion_blocker: Disposition is now documented, but the row is still best used as a non-core settlement sidecar unless a synchronized authoritative-core rewrite is explicitly chosen.
shared_reuse_state.observatory_hook: FTC settlement-sidecar panel; human-replacement-marketing and business-opportunity enforcement view; authority-depth dashboard.

title: CNIL warns sports club over proposed facial-recognition checks for commercial stadium bans
summary: Official French CNIL warning-page candidate on proposed facial-recognition use by a sports club for commercial stadium-ban enforcement, preserved as a bounded non-U.S./non-UK biometric-governance and legality-posture row.

actors.name: Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL)

system_context.model_or_provider: proposed facial-recognition system for spectator identification
tools_or_permissions: identification of persons subject to commercial stadium bans

source_claims.claim: The CNIL page states that the President of the CNIL warned a sports club that a proposed facial-recognition system aimed at identifying persons subject to commercial stadium bans was not compliant with the GDPR and the French Data Protection Act.

The page states that the contemplated system relied on biometric-data processing and that, absent a special legislative or regulatory basis, use of such a system by a sports club for anti-terrorism purposes would be illicit.

The page states that if the club nevertheless implemented the system after the warning, it would expose itself to one or more corrective measures under the GDPR and the French Data Protection Act, including a pecuniary sanction.

legal_normative_notes: Use the CNIL warning-page posture only; do not infer that the contemplated system was actually deployed or that a pecuniary sanction was imposed.
Preserve the French original for legality wording and do not widen the page into a general facial-recognition prohibition beyond the stated sports-club setting and current legal-framework analysis.
The row improves non-U.S./non-UK official authority breadth, but it remains page-summary depth unless a direct warning decision or companion document is later captured.
remediation_or_response: The CNIL page states that the club was warned that the proposed processing could not lawfully be implemented under the current legal framework.
The page states that implementation despite the warning would expose the club to corrective measures, including a possible pecuniary sanction.

manuscript_anchor_notes: Use for bounded regulator-warning wording on proposed facial-recognition checks for commercial stadium bans and the legality ceiling stated by CNIL.
Keep the warning-page posture explicit and do not convert the row into proof of deployment, adjudicated liability, or a final monetary sanction.

review_notes: Direct public CNIL warning page captured and hashed on-host; no direct warning-PDF or separate sanction document was claimed found in this bounded pass.
Row is kept as an authoritative candidate to widen non-U.S./non-UK official authority breadth without silently changing the shared authoritative-core denominator.
provenance.route_note: Live CNIL page rechecked for publication-date integrity; page-visible article date is 18 février 2021, while newer dates on-page belong to related-content cards.
